preparation_method: dressed with lemon juice and spices

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical side serving of kachumbar is approximately 100 g, equivalent to a small bowl served alongside a main Indian meal such as biryani or curry.

nutrient_source.title: Kachumber Salad — composite raw vegetable salad; values derived from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) component data for cucumber, tomato, onion, coriander leaves, and lemon juice in standard proportions
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single ICMR-NIN entry exists for kachumbar as a composite dish. Values estimated from ICMR-NIN 2017 component data using a standard recipe proportion: cucumber 40%, tomato 30%, onion 20%, coriander leaves 5%, lemon juice 4%, salt and cumin 1%. Energy, macros, fibre, sodium, and micronutrients calculated by weighted average of component per-100g values from ICMR-NIN tables.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — raw non-starchy vegetables (cucumber, tomato, onion composite)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for kachumbar as a composite dish. GI estimated at 15 based on the University of Sydney GI Database values for raw non-starchy vegetables: cucumber (GI ~15), tomato (GI ~15), and onion (GI ~10). The dish is predominantly raw low-GI vegetables with negligible starch content. Weighted composite GI estimated at 15, classified as low (≤55). GL per 100g serving = (15 × 5.4) / 100 = 0.81, rounded to 0.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium from added salt; high potassium from vegetables
hypertension.guidance: Kachumbar is a low-calorie raw vegetable salad with moderate sodium (~180 mg/100g from added salt); individuals with hypertension should request reduced salt preparation and benefit from the potassium content (~210 mg/100g) which supports blood pressure regulation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~15) and low carbohydrate content (5.4 g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kachumbar has a very low glycemic index of approximately 15 and a glycemic load of less than 1 per serving, making it an excellent side dish for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as it contributes negligible glycemic impact while providing dietary fibre and micronutrients.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat, zero cholesterol, high dietary fibre and antioxidant vitamins
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Kachumbar is a heart-healthy side dish with negligible saturated fat (0.04 g/100g), zero cholesterol, and meaningful vitamin C (~18 mg/100g) and folate (~22 mcg/100g) content; regular consumption as part of a balanced diet supports cardiovascular health.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kachumbar (also spelled Kachumber or Kuchumber) is a traditional Indian raw vegetable salad made from finely chopped cucumber, tomato, and onion, seasoned with fresh green chilli, coriander leaves, lemon juice, salt, and ground cumin. It is a staple accompaniment in Indian cuisine, commonly served alongside biryani, dal, curry, or grilled meats. The salad is prepared entirely raw with no cooking, making it a minimally processed, nutrient-dense side dish. Its texture is crisp and refreshing, with a bright, tangy flavour from the lemon juice and a mild heat from the green chilli. Nutritionally, kachumbar is very low in calories (~28 kcal/100g), low in fat (0.2 g/100g), and provides modest dietary fibre (1.4 g/100g), vitamin C (~18 mg/100g), and potassium (~210 mg/100g). Its glycemic index is very low (~15), making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The dish is naturally vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. In Singapore, kachumbar is served at Indian restaurants such as Kinara at Boat Quay as a side dish or starter. Clinically, it is a beneficial addition to cardiometabolic diets due to its low energy density, negligible saturated fat, and antioxidant micronutrient profile.
common_user_queries: Indian salad with cucumber and tomato

confidence_reason: No single ICMR-NIN or HPB entry exists for kachumbar as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN 2017 component data using standard recipe proportions. GI is estimated from University of Sydney GI Database values for constituent raw non-starchy vegetables. Confidence is medium because the estimation methodology is sound and sourced from approved primary sources, but direct measurement of the composite dish is not available.
notes: Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN 2017 component data for cucumber, tomato, onion, coriander leaves, and lemon juice in standard proportions (cucumber 40%, tomato 30%, onion 20%, coriander 5%, lemon juice 4%, salt/cumin 1%). Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN 2017 tables for these vegetable components. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 as this is a plant-based dish containing no animal products; confirmed zero by nature (naturally zero). Vitamin D is 0.0 as raw vegetables contain no meaningful vitamin D; naturally zero. Cholesterol is 0.0 as this is a plant-based dish; naturally zero. Trans fat is 0.0 as no hydrogenated fats are used; naturally zero. GI estimated at 15 based on University of Sydney GI Database analogous values for raw non-starchy vegetables. Sodium value (~180 mg/100g) reflects typical added salt in restaurant preparation; home preparation with less salt would reduce sodium significantly. Food served at Kinara - Boat Quay, Singapore (field survey source). FoodOn match is closest_parent (vegetable salad food product); no exact FoodOn term for kachumbar exists. FoodEx2 code A00LC used as closest parent category.
